topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. My hands, already clammy, trembled as I clicked it open. The headline, the byline—it was all wrong. My meticulously crafted piece on urban planning, the one I’d poured months into, was credited to some stranger. A cold, heavy dread settled in my chest, squeezing the air from my lungs. I stared at the screen, the words blurring into an incomprehensible smear. A slow, burning sensation rose in my throat, making swallowing difficult. I felt a strange, hollow ache spreading through my limbs. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small confines of my apartment, running a hand repeatedly through my hair, desperate to shake off the sickening realization that my work, my voice, had been stolen.

[story 2]

I reread the publication confirmation, the official notice mocking me from the screen. Every sentence felt brittle, sharp edges scraping against my nerves. A deep sigh escaped me, a sound ragged and defeated. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight, and simply stared at the wall. My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the muscle twitching beneath my skin. It wasn't anger that surfaced, just a profound sense of deflation, as if the very ground beneath my feet had dissolved. I started methodically gathering my notes, stacking them into a neat, depressing pile, each document feeling like a betrayal. The silence in the room was oppressive, thick with unspoken disappointment.

[story 3]

When the notification finally registered, a wave of nausea washed over me. I gripped the edge of the desk until my knuckles turned white. The words on the screen swam before my eyes, mocking my efforts. A sudden, hot flush crept up my neck, staining my cheeks. I didn't move for a long time, just breathing in shallow, quick gasps. My gaze drifted to the window, seeing only the relentless, indifferent rush of city traffic below. A low, shaky sound escaped my lips—a sound devoid of energy. I slowly pushed away from the desk, the movement sluggish, as if moving through water. The sheer unfairness of it left me feeling utterly drained, hollowed out.
